speaker: interviewer
text: September 25, 1996. This is an interview with Bernhard Storch.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, that picture here which is in front of me is a picture of my father as an officer in the Russian army. And it was taken between the year of 1916 and 1918. I don't know exact date. But he was still in the-- in the military army then. He was on duty at that time. Shortly after that, he was wounded, and he spent a whole year recuperating in a hospital. And he, for his heroism, he got awarded three medals. I don't remember the distinction of the medals, but I know as a child we played with those medals. And you probably can see them on the uniform. They are pinned there, three medals. This is a picture of my uncle. The one which was-- I was apprenticing with, his wife, and four of my brothers, and one of my cousins. And that picture is made on the River Raba. We used to live above that river. You can see a bridge crossing over that river there. And it was done in 1937 during summer. It'd have to be in June 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you happen to have it now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I happen to have it because my aunt which lives in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Israel, she had it, and I got it through her. And I forgot to mention about, as far as my father's picture, she-- I got that picture also from my uncle because he had it. That picture was sent to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: The uncle in Brooklyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: The uncle in Brooklyn, yeah, his twin, my father's twin brother. I didn't have any pictures on me when I left home. This is my last family picture made together, just about a month before my father, unfortunately, passed away. And left to right there's my brother which is born in 1924-- December 6, 1924-- his name is [NON-ENGLISH]. My mother, of course. In the center is myself, always with a soccer ball, you can see that. Below myself is my youngest little brother. His name is Leon. And he was eight years old when I saw him the last time. My father, and on your right, extreme right, holding the arm over my-- on my father's shoulder is the middle brother of mine. His name is [NON-ENGLISH]. And the whole group of people, minus my father of course, is the last time which I saw was September 3rd, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: From whom did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got that picture from my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Brooklyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm sorry. No. This picture, I'm sorry, no. This picture I got from my aunt, from my aunt from Israel. He did not have that picture. OK. That picture is of my sister. This picture, it's a marriage performed in the ghetto. And it was done and in Tomaszow Mazowiecki ghetto. And it was done March 1, 1941. In the center of the picture, you would see a gentleman and the lady with a little bow on her, on her right shoulder. That's the couple who got married in the ghetto. And unfortunately, they did not survive the war. My sister-in-law was together, with my wife, in Bialystok in 1939. And unfortunately-- I mean unfortunately for her, but unfortunately she didn't survive. She had a boyfriend at that particular time. And when my wife got together with her two sisters, she decided to go home and marry her fiance. And of course, that was the end of that. 1941. They died in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you have this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I have through my uncle in St. Louis. My wife's uncle, actually, in St. Louis. He lives in St. Louis. The name is [NON-ENGLISH], and they got the picture to her. My mother-in-law sent that picture to him. OK. This is a picture of my fiance then. At that time, actually, when I got that picture, I didn't even dream that we get-- we would get eventually married. That's my picture of my wife. And I hid that picture. You see all these creases in that picture there through the entire war. When I left, my wife handed that picture to me-- my fiance, then, of course-- handed that picture to me. And, of course, I treasured that and carried it through-- throughout the war. And it was really-- that picture really was something that drove me to survive throughout the war. It's a beautiful picture. It was done in the back of my-- of the apartment houses where we were living.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in [NON-ENGLISH], in [NON-ENGLISH]. It was not an apartment house. It was a house from a woman which has two children. And her husband was in the war. And he was already quite a while-- she didn't get a letter from him in one year. And who knows if he survived or not. But anyway that was right there in [NON-ENGLISH] in a small, small home. Now, over here, the lady on the left is my aunt, my wife's sister actually. And on the right is my uncle. This is the lady, Mona Lehrfeld, and because she lived in Germany, because she was thrown out from Germany in 1938, she urged everybody to leave home. And because of her, really, everybody left. And that group of people which left would have-- minus that one uncle which was at that time detained by the Russians because they needed him-- the whole group of people survived. Now those two little graves which you can see, they lost a child, a little daughter. She was a baby. And they lost a child in Russia in 1942. And next to it is, again, another child. They lost a little girl. Her name is Rachel, [NON-ENGLISH]. And on the other side here, there's another tombstone which is a son from the other uncle, Uncle David Lehrfeld which is a brother of his, of him. And unfortunately, it was a meningitis epidemic in Russia at that particular time, that particular year. An awful lot of children and grownups died. So they buried next to each other. And I remember that from here when we buried them. It was middle of the winter. There was tremendous frost. We couldn't even open up deep enough the grave. And later, I went to the army. I don't know, I never asked my uncle if that child was reburied or not, because it was just a shallow grave. They couldn't dig any deeper because it was 60 degrees below zero. And both children are named Lehrfeld from both brothers. And September-- yeah, this is September, yeah, OK. I think it was later than that. But in any event, that picture was taken in September 1943. And it was taken in the forest of Smolensk. This is my cannon. And the fellow next to me, his name is [NON-ENGLISH], and he came from city Lodz. And he was in the telephone outfits. And we got very close together, and we really stick to each other. He survived. He survived the war. And as a matter of fact, after the war, I met his brother which also survived Auschwitz. He was transferred to Germany. And he was able to return back, go back home. He went on furlough in August. And that's how he met his brother. They survived. Now with this cannon, I was able to go all the way down from Soviet Union all the way down to Berlin. OK. That's November 18, 1945, in the city of Katowice. And the street where we used to live was Francisca, Street Francisca, which is French, Number 20. And that was our wedding day. And whatever you see on my wife, everything is borrowed. This suit, I made myself. But on the blouse, she wore a blouse, because you couldn't get a gown. So she wears a lace blouse. She borrowed that blouse from somebody. And somehow we improvised the head piece. You can see that. And with it, she had a navy skirt. And that was her wedding outfit. But the wedding was beautiful. We had altogether about 18 people, very close family. And it was performed by a rabbi. And I still have the marriage certificate with me, a religious marriage certificate, I have with me. The picture is good. The picture was originally black and white. One of my-- one of my customer's daughter was going in New York City to a art school. And I asked her about it. And she simply, she simply drawed it in color herself for me. It's beautiful. OK. This picture was taken actually Fort Tryon Park, believe it or not, in New York City, in Washington Heights there, and Riverdale. And that's myself, and then my wife there, and then my little boy which at that time, that was in 1954, he was a couple months old. As well as I remember. It was in 1954, and my daughter, was at that time six years old. OK. I will describe you left to right. And this picture was taken November 18, 1995, our 50th Wedding Anniversary. And the kids treat us to our anniversary. Most of my grandson-- the big guy there-- they treat us to an anniversary party. And it was in Orlando, Florida. And the reason for Orlando is [INAUDIBLE] there was a bat mitzvah there on Friday. And the families were there. So everybody attended that particular wedding. So we had almost three times as many people as at our original wedding. Left to right is my daughter-in-law Janet, my wife Ruth, myself, Bernhard, and the tall fellow is my grandson Robert, Robert Minh Morris, my daughter Gita Morris. Left to right my grandchild, my granddaughter, Rebecca. She was at that particular time almost 6 and a half. And then my grandson, Joshua, and he was almost nine then, nine and a half, yeah. Thank you. That's beautiful. OK. Left to right is a medal which I was awarded for each one which I was involved in liberating and crossing the river Oder, the river Nice-- that's in Germany-- and then involved in liberating Baltic Sea, around the area of [NON-ENGLISH]. This medal, this medal was given by Marshal Stalin. And it was for liberating the city of Warsaw, the capital city of Poland, the city of Warsaw. I have the same medal from Washington which is given by the Polish army also. The next one here is a medal for taking part in liberating the city Berlin, and victory over Berlin. That was also given by the Stalin regime. This medal here is just simply awarded for victory over Germany, originating in May 9th , 1945. OK. That's a citation. And part of the medal was given by-- it was given on the 2nd of May 1945. And the number of that citation is 359. And it was given by the Red Army Chief, Marshal Stalin, for talking part for-- to the victors of Berlin, simply to the victors of Berlin. And it's my name there, Sztorch Bernhard. And it's [INAUDIBLE] under that particular time. And it's signed by my regiment commander. And I have about eight or nine, this same kind of certificates from that-- from that period, from that period. OK. This is a cross for Bravery and Gallantry in the Field of Glory. And it was originally given to me in March of 1945. And the reason for it was for the strike to German tanks, two target German tanks, while they tried to break out through the lines. Unfortunately for them, but fortunately for us, they got through the infantry lines, but they did not make it through the artillery lines. And that's why I was able to destroy two of them. Now this cross-- this cross is the third highest given by the Polish government. And only myself and the captain of the battery received one on that particular day. And that's the certificates. And that medal was given to me here on-- originally, it was sent from Warsaw on the 23rd of November 1993. But I received it in New York in April of 1994 from the Polish consulate. There was a big ceremony. We were invited. And there was a lot of things to be done. There were over 300 people in that place. So it was a very nice thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please introduce us to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. On my left, my lovely wife of 51 years, almost, Ruth Sztorch. And my son, Larry Storch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like to say about your father and making the tape today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very glad that he's doing it to preserve his story so that my children and future generations can know something of what happened during the war. Those of us who weren't there can't really know. No matter how many times we hear or see pictures of it, we get only an echo of what occurred. But hearing that echo from somebody who was there helps make sure that the story doesn't get lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you care to add anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: You'd better. Otherwise, I'm not going to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, what can I add to what my husband has already told you? I think he is-- he has been pretty elaborate. And I think he's doing very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
